g.data-narration: Welcome to Data Representation Revisited. This module covers how computers store and process different types of data.
Data representation is how computers store, interpret, and process different types of data. It's fundamental to all computing.
g.text: Computers use binary (0s and 1s) to store
helps write efficient code and debug issues.

g.data-narration: We'll explore four key topics: number systems, signed numbers, floating-point, and character encoding. Each is essential.
Number systems include binary (base-2), octal (base-8), and hexadecimal (base-16). Each serves different purposes in computing.
Converting between number systems is essential. Binary to decimal uses positional values. Hex groups 4 bits for compact representation.
Signed numbers represent positive and negative integers. Sign-magnitude uses the MSB as a sign bit, with remaining bits for magnitude.
1's complement flips all bits to represent negatives. Still has the dual-zero problem, requiring end-around carry for addition.
2's complement is the standard! Invert bits and add 1. Has unique zero, simpler arithmetic, and asymmetric range.
Let's convert -87 to 8-bit 2's complement: 87 in binary is 01010111, invert to get 10101000, then add 1 to get 10101001.
Floating-point represents real numbers using scientific notation. IEEE 754 is the universal standard with 32-bit single and 64-bit double precision.
IEEE 754 single precision has three parts: 1-bit sign (positive/negative), 8-bit exponent (biased by 127), and 23-bit mantissa with implicit leading 1.
Converting -6.75 to IEEE 754: Binary is 110.11, normalize to 1.1011 × 2². Sign=1, exponent=2+127=129, mantissa=1011.
Character encoding maps characters to numbers. ASCII uses 7 bits (128 chars). Unicode extends to 16-32 bits for global languages. UTF-8 is most common.
Fixed-point representation places the decimal point at a fixed position. In Q8.8 format (16-bit), 5.5 equals 1408 in decimal (5.5 × 256).
Remember these key formulas: 2's complement range is -2^(n-1) to 2^(n-1)-1. IEEE 754 value is (-1)^S × 1.F × 2^(E-bias). Bias is 127 for single precision.
Exam tips: Always add bias to exponent in IEEE 754. Remember implicit leading 1 in mantissa. Watch for asymmetric range in 2's complement. Watch for overflow!
g.text: ✓ Add bias to exponent in IEEE 754
